prompt:
Ticket from the scheduler rewrite. Our dispatch loop keeps jobs in a plain slice and re-sorts on every reprioritization; at 10k queued jobs the p99 dispatch latency is embarrassing, and container/heap alone doesn't cut it because ops constantly bumps or cancels SPECIFIC jobs by id mid-queue.

I need an indexed min-priority queue: package ipq, root of module go-indexpq, stdlib only.

  func New() *Queue
  func (q *Queue) Len() int
  func (q *Queue) Push(key string, priority int) error
  func (q *Queue) Peek() (string, int, error)
  func (q *Queue) Pop() (string, int, error)
  func (q *Queue) UpdatePriority(key string, priority int) error
  func (q *Queue) Remove(key string) error
  var ErrEmpty, ErrDuplicateKey, ErrUnknownKey

Semantics I need nailed down:
- Min queue: lowest priority number dispatches first. Pop returns and removes the front; Peek returns it without removing.
- Keys are unique while enqueued. Push of a key already in the queue: ErrDuplicateKey, and the existing entry stays exactly as it was.
- UpdatePriority and Remove address any enqueued job by key in better-than-linear time — that's the whole point — and return ErrUnknownKey for absent keys. Failed calls change nothing.
- Ties break FIFO by ARRIVAL: among equal priorities, whoever was Pushed first pops first. Deterministic, always. UpdatePriority does not reset a job's arrival order — bump a job up and back down and it still ranks by its original Push among equals. Remove followed by a fresh Push of the same key IS a new arrival, it goes to the back of its priority class.
- Pop/Peek on an empty queue: ErrEmpty.

The drain order must be fully deterministic — the suite drains a couple hundred jobs and compares the exact sequence. Interior removals must not corrupt ordering (this is where our last homegrown attempt died).

Acceptance contract is ipq_test.go — do not edit it. go test -race -timeout 30s ./... must pass.
